title: Data & Statistics
objective: Students will explore data & statistics concepts including Sorting Data into Categories, Pictograms and tally charts (age 6+), Pictograms and tally charts and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Sorting Data into Categories. Get ready for an adventure!
Organise and represent data with up to three categories by counting objects in each category and sorting categories by quantity
Time to try this out: Sort a set of objects into 2-3 given categories and count each group
Can you look at a collection of objects — like different coloured counters — sort them into up to three groups, count each group, and organise the results into a simple chart?
Great job! Today we learned about Sorting Data into Categories. Sort a set of objects into 2-3 given categories and count each group Create a simple table or list showing category names and counts
Let's explore Pictograms and tally charts (age 6+)! Have you ever wondered about this?

Read, write, and use the vocabulary of data collection and display — data, tally, tally chart, frequency, frequency table, survey, pictogram, bar chart, axis/axes, scale, label, category, discrete data, continuous data, line graph, pie chart — and apply these terms when collecting, organising, and presenting data
Let me show you something cool. Correctly label the axes of a bar chart including a title, axis labels, and scale
If you wanted to show you how many books different children in their class read last month, could they draw a bar chart, label the axes, and explain what each bar represents?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Pictograms and tally charts (age 6+): Correctly label the axes of a bar chart including a title, axis labels, and scale Distinguish between discrete data (counted) and continuous data (measured) with an example of each
Let's explore Pictograms and tally charts! Have you ever wondered about this?
Interpret and construct simple pictograms, tally charts, block diagrams, and simple tables
Time to try this out: Read a pictogram where each symbol represents one item
Can you read a simple chart or pictogram — like one showing how many children chose each favourite fruit — and answer questions like "which was most popular"?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Pictograms and tally charts: Read a pictogram where each symbol represents one item Construct a tally chart from collected data
Have you ever thought about Sorting into categories (age 6+)? Let's find out together!
Interpret categorical data by asking and answering questions about totals, how many in each category, and how many more or less one category has than another
Time to try this out: Answer 'how many?' for each category in a data set
If you looks at a chart showing how many children chose each flavour of ice cream, can they answer questions like "how many chose chocolate?" and "how many more chose vanilla than strawberry?"
Great job! Today we learned about Sorting into categories (age 6+). Answer 'how many?' for each category in a data set Calculate the total number of data points across all categories
